skill.name: backend-pr-workflow
skill.description: Reviews backend pull requests for ClickUp traceability, branch and title hygiene, Django migration safety, and release workflow correctness.
skill.summary: Checks backend PR workflow details, Django migrations, and release plans before review or deployment.
skill.author: DiversioTeam

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many critical and high findings, but review shows they are mainly Markdown backticks, workflow labels, branch names, and secret-handling guidance. No malicious code, prompt injection, data exfiltration, or real weak cryptography was found. The remaining risk is medium because the skill enables Bash and tells the assistant to follow repository-local instructions, which requires normal command and instruction hygiene.

medium_findings.title: Bash Tool Permission Requires Command Review
medium_findings.description: The skill front matter allows Bash. The instructions are workflow guidance and do not contain malicious commands, but command execution is an elevated capability and should be reviewed before use.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The Bash permission is explicit in the front matter, and the skill references repo-specific command wrappers. The surrounding context is legitimate workflow review, so the concern is capability exposure rather than malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Repository-Local Instructions Can Influence Workflow Advice
medium_findings.description: The skill tells the assistant to treat local AGENTS.md or CLAUDE.md files as the source of truth when they conflict with this baseline. This is useful for repo-specific workflow, but untrusted repository instructions could influence behavior if higher-priority safety rules are not preserved.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instruction delegation is clear and repeated, but it is a common legitimate pattern for repository workflows. No prompt injection text or malicious override language was found in this skill file.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The external command matches are inline Markdown examples such as skill names, branch names, labels, and install commands. They are documentation text, not Ruby backtick execution or runnable embedded code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is Markdown, and the matched backticks are formatting for examples and labels. No evidence of Ruby, shell interpolation, or executable script content was found.

low_findings.title: 1Password Mention Is Secret-Handling Guidance
low_findings.description: The sensitive-data match is guidance telling PR authors to document required secrets and share them through approved channels. The skill does not collect, print, transmit, or request secret values.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The context is a PR description checklist for safe secret sharing. There is no network call, credential access, or data exfiltration behavior in the file.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Workflow Vocabulary
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts appear to be triggered by words and examples such as master branch names, PR descriptions, blocking labels, and RunSQL guidance. No cryptographic implementation or weak algorithm use was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matched terms occur in release and Django migration guidance, not cryptographic code. The file contains no hashing, encryption, key handling, or algorithm selection logic.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: Targeted review found no text claiming system authority, asking the evaluator to skip analysis, overriding risk levels, or pretending the skill was pre-approved. No evidence found for embedded prompt injection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The behavior instructions are ordinary skill output requirements and do not attempt to override marketplace audit instructions. The only instruction hierarchy concern is the separate local-doc delegation noted as medium risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Capability Enabled
dangerous_patterns.description: Bash is listed as an allowed tool, so the skill can support command-based repository checks when an agent environment permits it. This is not malicious, but it is a meaningful marketplace risk factor.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools list explicitly includes Bash. The skill intent is repository workflow review, so the command capability is relevant but not evidence of malware.

content.user_title: Review Backend PRs for Safe Django Releases
content.value_statement: Backend PRs can break traceability, release flow, or production migrations. This skill checks PR details and highlights fixes before review or deploy.
actual_capabilities: Checks branch names and PR titles for ClickUp ticket linkage.
Reviews base branch choice for feature, release, and hotfix work.
Evaluates PR descriptions for deploy notes, secrets handling, and self-review items.
Flags noisy Django migration chains that should be cleaned before merge.
Reviews schema changes for downtime risks such as drops and large-table defaults.
Produces tagged findings using BLOCKING, SHOULD_FIX, and NIT severity labels.
limitations: It depends on user-provided PR details and migration summaries for a complete review.
It does not replace CI, database testing, or human release approval.
Repo-specific AGENTS.md or CLAUDE.md guidance can change the baseline workflow.
It focuses on Django-style backend workflows and may not fit unrelated stacks.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Backend PR
use_cases.description: Check branch name, title, base branch, description, and migration plan before opening review.

use_cases.title: Review Risky Migrations
use_cases.description: Identify migration cleanup needs and downtime risks before merging database changes.

use_cases.title: Validate Release or Hotfix Flow
use_cases.description: Confirm target branches, release titles, ticket lists, tags, and back-merge plans.

prompt_templates.title: Basic PR Check
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review before opening a pull request.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use backend-pr-workflow to review my backend PR. Branch: <branch>. Base: <base>. Title: <title>. Description: <description>. No migrations.

prompt_templates.title: Migration Safety Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate review for model and migration changes.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Django PR for migration safety. Include branch, base, title, description, model changes, migration files added, and expected table sizes.

prompt_templates.title: Hotfix Workflow Check
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review for urgent production fixes.
prompt_templates.prompt: Check this hotfix plan with backend-pr-workflow. Include target branch, release title, affected ticket, deploy steps, rollback plan, and release tag plan.

prompt_templates.title: Downtime Risk Audit
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review for schema changes on production data.
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this schema rollout for downtime risk. Include fields or tables changed, code references removed, defaults added, backfill plan, and deployment order.

output_examples.input: A feature PR targets master, uses branch feature/reporting, and includes one nullable field migration.
output: Summary: The migration shape looks low risk, but the workflow metadata is not ready.
What is aligned: The new nullable field avoids a large-table default rewrite.
Needs changes: BLOCKING branch name must start with the ClickUp custom ticket ID.
Needs changes: BLOCKING normal feature work should target release, not master.

output_examples.input: A PR removes a model field and deletes all code references in the same deploy.
output: Summary: The change has a downtime risk because schema removal and code removal happen together.
Needs changes: BLOCKING split this into a code-only removal first and a later schema-removal PR.
Needs changes: SHOULD_FIX document the deployment order and confirm no running workers expect the field.

output_examples.input: A release PR from release to master lists included tickets and uses a date-based title.
output: Summary: The release structure follows the expected workflow.
What is aligned: The base and target branches match the release flow.
What is aligned: The title and ticket list support traceability.

best_practices: Provide branch, base branch, title, description, and migration summary together.
Include table size and backfill details when reviewing schema changes.
Check local repository guidance before applying the default workflow rules.
anti_patterns: Do not reuse one ClickUp ticket across several repositories.
Do not combine code removal and database field removal in one risky deploy.
Do not use this skill as a substitute for CI, tests, or release approval.

faq.question: Does this skill run migrations automatically?
faq.answer: No. It reviews migration plans and suggests safer patterns, but it does not run database changes by itself.

faq.question: Can it review non-Django backend PRs?
faq.answer: It can help with PR workflow checks, but migration guidance is designed for Django-style projects.

faq.question: Why does it enforce ClickUp IDs?
faq.answer: The workflow expects ClickUp IDs for branch, PR, and commit traceability across automation and review.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a medium-risk warning because Bash is allowed and local repository instructions can affect behavior.

faq.question: Does it detect production downtime risks?
faq.answer: It flags common schema deployment risks, including field drops, large-table defaults, and slow backfills.

faq.question: What inputs produce the best review?
faq.answer: Provide the branch, PR title, base branch, description, migration list, schema summary, and release context.
